metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace bare `wc -l` proving commands with shell assertions that machine-enforce the bounds, e.g. `lines=$(wc -l < src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py) && [ "$lines" -ge 100 ] && [ "$lines" -le 800 ] && echo ok` (and analogously `[ … -le 800 ]` for AC2).
evidence: 'New leaf src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py is in [100,800] LOC. Verified by: wc -l …' — wc -l exits 0 regardless of count
Taxonomy item 5: missing cardinality assertion when the criterion names a count
findings.finding: AC1 and AC2 proving commands (`wc -l …`) output a raw number but do not machine-enforce the stated bounds ([100,800] and ≤800 respectively); if the leaf is accidentally under 100 LOC or the residual file stays over 800, the command still exits 0 and the AC silently passes.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (`wc -l src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py`) and AC2 (`wc -l src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py`)
scenarios: New leaf ends up at 90 lines (just the 6 functions with thin docstrings); wc -l outputs 90 and the executor must notice the violation manually rather than the command failing.
Residual _processors.py slips to 810 lines; wc -l exits 0, AC2 is marked done.
findings.suggested_fix:
AC1: `lines=$(wc -l < src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py) && [ "$lines" -ge 100 ] && [ "$lines" -le 800 ] && echo "ok ($lines lines)"`
AC2: `lines=$(wc -l < src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py) && [ "$lines" -le 800 ] && echo "ok ($lines lines)"`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'true leaf / no other callers' premise: document that `_fold_author_attribution` is also called by `process_create` (line 150), `process_comment` (line 394), and `process_revert` (line 520), and that `attestation_kind` and `_bootstrap_genesis_keyring` are also called by `process_snapshot` and `process_create` — so `_processors.py` must both re-export AND actively import these symbols from the leaf (not only provide `# noqa: F401` stubs).
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:150 — _fold_author_attribution(state, event) inside process_create (not in the 6-symbol extract list)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:394 — _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event) inside process_comment (not in the extract list)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:520 — _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event) inside process_revert (not in the extract list)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:834 — attestation_kind(sig.get('manifest'), {}) inside process_snapshot (not in the extract list)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:852 — _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, ...) inside process_snapshot (not in the extract list)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:213 — _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, ...) inside process_create (not in the extract list)
Plan quote: 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' — contradicted by the above call sites remaining in _processors.py
findings.finding: The plan claims the 6-symbol cluster 'reference only each other (a true leaf)' and that '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms.' Both assertions are factually incomplete: _fold_author_attribution is also called by process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520) — all of which remain in _processors.py after the extract. Additionally, attestation_kind is called by process_snapshot (line 834) and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring by process_snapshot (line 852) and process_create (line 213), none of which are in the extract set. The cycle-prevention rationale therefore rests on an incomplete picture of the cross-cluster call graph. While the re-export mechanism still works (callers in _processors.py will use _fold_author_attribution via the module-level re-import), the implementor needs to know that _processors.py will have ACTIVE in-module call sites for 3 of the 6 extracted symbols — not just re-export stubs — and must arrange the imports accordingly without creating a circular import.
findings.location: Plan body — 'These reference only each other (a true leaf): process_key_event calls _rederive_keyring_keys; process_signature calls attestation_kind and _fold_author_attribution. _fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf, else a back-edge cycle forms.'
scenarios: Implementor moves _fold_author_attribution to the leaf and removes it from _processors.py's namespace, breaking process_create, process_comment, and process_revert which call it directly and are not re-exported.
Implementor adds only `# noqa: F401` re-export lines but forgets to also import _fold_author_attribution for the in-module use sites, causing a NameError at runtime.

A circular-import analysis is done on the false belief that _processors.py has no call sites for the extracted symbols; the actual import graph (where _processors.py both uses and re-exports the leaf's symbols) requires explicit verification.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' with: 'Within the leaf the call graph is self-contained (process_key_event→_rederive_keyring_keys; process_signature→attestation_kind+_fold_author_attribution). However _fold_author_attribution, attestation_kind, and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring are ALSO called by processors staying in _processors.py (process_create at line 150+213, process_comment at line 394, process_revert at line 520, process_snapshot at lines 834+852). _processors.py must therefore import these 3 symbols from _processors_identity at the top of the module (for use by the remaining callers) and then re-export all 6 with # noqa: F401 — the re-import lines serve both purposes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the 'stdlib-free' constraint for _processors_identity.py, or restate the constraint accurately (e.g., 'no intra-package back-edges' rather than 'stdlib-free') and confirm the existing lint/import-linter step covers it.
evidence: 'NEW pure, stdlib-free leaf' — stated design constraint
Absence: no AC contains a grep, ruff rule, or import-linter check for stdlib imports in _processors_identity.py
findings.finding: The plan declares the new leaf 'stdlib-free' as a design constraint but no AC verifies this property; an implementer who inadvertently imports a stdlib module (e.g., hashlib, dataclasses) in _processors_identity.py would pass all ACs.
findings.location: Plan body, first paragraph: 'NEW pure, stdlib-free leaf src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py'
scenarios: The 'stdlib-free' invariant silently breaks if a moved function uses, e.g., hashlib or typing, making future dependency-graph reasoning incorrect.
A reviewer cannot confirm the leaf property from CI output alone.
findings.suggested_fix: If 'stdlib-free' means 'no rebar-internal back-edges' (i.e., only rebar imports are to siblings already imported by the leaf cluster), clarify the wording. If it literally means no stdlib, add: 'python -c "import ast, sys; tree=ast.parse(open(\"src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py\").read()); [sys.exit(1) for n in ast.walk(tree) if isinstance(n, ast.Import) and any(a.name in sys.stdlib_module_names for a in n.names)]"' or an equivalent ruff/import-linter check.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `wc -l src/rebar/reducer/_processors_identity.py` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
verify command `wc -l src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable

verify command `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` greps the unanchored word '_processors_keys' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review__processors_keys'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"_processors_keys":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind are ACTIVELY USED (not just re-exported for back-compat) by process_create, process_comment, process_revert, and process_snapshot remaining in _processors.py — the import from the new leaf is required for both internal use and external back-compat, so the '# noqa: F401' annotation should not be applied to these three symbols (only to symbols with no remaining callers in _processors.py, if any).
evidence: process_create (line 150): _fold_author_attribution(state, event) — stays in _processors.py
process_comment (line 394): _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event) — stays in _processors.py
process_revert (line 520): _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event) — stays in _processors.py
process_create (line 213): _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, ...) — stays in _processors.py
process_snapshot (line 852): _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, ...) — stays in _processors.py
process_snapshot (line 834): attestation_kind(sig.get('manifest'), {}) — stays in _processors.py
Plan only identifies process_signature as the caller motivating _fold_author_attribution's move, missing the 3 remaining callers
findings.finding: The plan describes the 6 moved symbols as 'a true leaf: reference only each other', but _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind are ALSO called by functions that STAY in _processors.py (process_create calls both _fold_author_attribution and _bootstrap_genesis_keyring; process_comment and process_revert call _fold_author_attribution; process_snapshot calls both _bootstrap_genesis_keyring and attestation_kind). The plan frames the re-import from the new leaf as a back-compat-only concern ('# noqa: F401 so rebar.reducer._processors.<symbol> keeps resolving'), but those same imports are also required for the intra-module callers. An implementer reading '# noqa: F401' as 'unused re-export only' could accidentally suppress the import or mis-scope it, breaking the 4 remaining processors that depend on these symbols at runtime.
findings.location: Plan body: 'These reference only each other (a true leaf)' / 're-exports them (# noqa: F401) so rebar.reducer._processors.<symbol> keeps resolving'
scenarios: Implementer adds 'from ._processors_identity import process_key_event, process_signature, attestation_kind, _fold_author_attribution, _rederive_keyring_keys, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring  # noqa: F401' but the linter or a later cleanup removes the '# noqa' and flags the import as unused (since the only visible uses in _processors.py ARE the active calls — ruff may not flag them as unused, but if the import is restructured into two lines with 'back-compat only' vs 'used internally', a maintainer removes the wrong half)
Implementer treats the re-export block as purely external-API glue and accidentally places it in __init__.py instead of _processors.py, breaking the intra-module call sites
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's MOVE section, add: '_processors.py imports _fold_author_attribution, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, and attestation_kind for ACTIVE USE by process_create, process_comment, process_revert, and process_snapshot (not just for back-compat re-export); these three imports must NOT carry # noqa: F401 since ruff will not flag them as unused. Only _rederive_keyring_keys, process_key_event, and process_signature are purely re-exported (no remaining direct callers in _processors.py) and take the # noqa: F401 annotation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that _fold_author_attribution has 3 additional callers in _processors.py (process_create, process_comment, process_revert) and that the re-import from _processors_identity into _processors.py must be a live module-scope import (not only a # noqa: F401 re-export guard), so those call sites continue to resolve.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 150: _fold_author_attribution(state, event) inside process_create (NOT in the 6 named symbols)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 394: _fold_author_attribution(_entry, event) inside process_comment (NOT in the 6 named symbols)
src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 520: _fold_author_attribution(_revert_record, event) inside process_revert (NOT in the 6 named symbols)
Plan says 'process_signature calls attestation_kind and _fold_author_attribution' as the sole rationale for moving _fold_author_attribution
findings.finding: The plan's dependency rationale for _fold_author_attribution is factually incomplete: it states 'process_signature calls … _fold_author_attribution' as if process_signature is the sole internal caller, but process_create (line 150), process_comment (line 394), and process_revert (line 520) in _processors.py also call _fold_author_attribution. All three of those functions stay in _processors.py after the move. For the re-import in _processors.py to serve those 3 callers (not just provide an external back-compat re-export), the import line must be in module scope (not just a # noqa: F401 comment added for ruff-silence) — an implementer who reads 'its caller = process_signature' may add _fold_author_attribution only to the external re-export guard and miss the load-bearing module-scope import needed by process_create/process_comment/process_revert.
findings.location: Plan body — 'MOVE (by name)' section; '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel with process_signature (its caller) into the leaf'
scenarios: Implementer adds _fold_author_attribution to a re-export-only TYPE_CHECKING or noqa block but not to the live module-scope import; process_create/process_comment/process_revert raise NameError at runtime.

Implementer skips moving _fold_author_attribution to the leaf (leaving it in _processors.py) because the rationale 'its caller is process_signature' seems wrong for the other callers — but process_signature is now in the leaf and imports _fold_author_attribution from _processors.py, creating a back-edge cycle.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the rationale sentence to: '_fold_author_attribution MUST travel into the leaf because process_signature (in the leaf) calls it; _processors.py re-imports it at module scope (not merely as a noqa re-export) so the 3 additional callers there — process_create, process_comment, and process_revert — continue to resolve without any call-site edits.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document that process_snapshot (line 852 of _processors.py) also calls _bootstrap_genesis_keyring, so that symbol's re-import into _processors.py is understood as load-bearing for two non-leaf callers (process_create and process_snapshot), not just for back-compat re-export.
evidence: src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py line 852: _bootstrap_genesis_keyring(state, str(state.get('created_at') or '')) inside process_snapshot
Plan enumerates no caller of _bootstrap_genesis_keyring other than the implicit reference to process_create via process_key_event context
findings.finding: process_snapshot (staying in _processors.py) calls _bootstrap_genesis_keyring at line 852, but this caller is not enumerated anywhere in the plan. The plan only contextually implies process_create as the caller of the keys cluster. An implementer who audits cross-references before coding may miss that process_snapshot also depends on the moving symbol.
findings.location: Plan body — 'MOVE (by name)' section; description of _bootstrap_genesis_keyring's callers
scenarios: Implementer scans for callers of _bootstrap_genesis_keyring before moving it, finds process_create (obvious) but misses process_snapshot, and incorrectly concludes the function is not load-bearing for the snapshot migration path.

If the re-import of _bootstrap_genesis_keyring into _processors.py is accidentally omitted, the snapshot keyring-migration branch (lines 850-852) raises NameError on first SNAPSHOT replay of any identity ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: '_bootstrap_genesis_keyring is called from both process_create (line 213) and process_snapshot (line 852), both staying in _processors.py; the module-scope re-import is required for both.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a _processors_identity.py row to the docs/architecture.md offenders/seams table OR explicitly state in the plan body (and add an AC) that the new leaf is intentionally omitted from that table (e.g., because it is under the ceiling and needs no tracking).
evidence: 'The sub-floor _processors_keys.py row is stale/superseded and is DELETED by this ticket'
AC 5: '! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md'
Absence: no plan text mentions adding a _processors_identity.py row to the offenders table, and no AC checks for its presence
findings.finding: The plan describes deleting the stale _processors_keys.py row from docs/architecture.md but never mentions adding (or explicitly omitting) a _processors_identity.py row. AC 5 only verifies the deletion; no AC verifies the architecture doc is updated to reflect the new leaf's existence.
scenarios: The architecture doc becomes stale in the opposite direction — the new leaf is undocumented, and a future reviewer cannot determine whether _processors_identity.py was intentionally omitted from tracking or simply forgotten.
A future 'add to offenders table' ticket duplicates work already implicitly done here.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'docs/architecture.md offenders table contains a row for _processors_identity.py. Verified by: grep -q _processors_identity docs/architecture.md'. Alternatively, if the table only tracks over-cap modules, add a sentence to the plan body: '_processors_identity.py is under the 800-LOC ceiling and therefore requires no offenders-table row.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a row (or equivalent entry) for `_processors_identity.py` to the `docs/architecture.md` offenders/module table when deleting the stale `_processors_keys.py` row, so the architecture doc reflects the actual post-refactor module set.
evidence: Plan states: 'The stale offenders-table row is deleted from docs/architecture.md. Verified by: ! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md'
Plan states: 'DEVIATION from the docs/architecture.md offenders row: … The sub-floor _processors_keys.py row is stale/superseded and is DELETED by this ticket'
No AC item or plan prose adds a new row/entry for _processors_identity.py to architecture.md or any design doc
T5e: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update' — the new leaf module is not registered anywhere persistent
findings.finding: The stale docs/architecture.md offenders-table row for _processors_keys.py is deleted by AC-5, but no corresponding row or mention of the new _processors_identity.py leaf is added to the architecture doc, leaving the offenders table (and any module-inventory section) silent about the newly created module.
findings.location: AC item 5 / docs/architecture.md offenders table / plan body 'DEVIATION from the docs/architecture.md offenders row'
scenarios: A future contributor reads docs/architecture.md looking for the module inventory and finds _processors_identity.py neither listed nor described, leading to confusion about its purpose and origin.
The module-size contract test passes but architecture.md is silently incomplete, defeating its purpose as a navigational aid.
findings.suggested_fix: In the same diff that deletes the `_processors_keys.py` row, add a new row for `_processors_identity.py` in docs/architecture.md — e.g.: `| reducer/_processors_identity.py | identity & signed-attestation fold cluster | ~130 LOC |` — and add an AC verification step such as `grep -q '_processors_identity' docs/architecture.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before merging the leaf, update the parent epic's architecture.md remedy row (or add a note to the parent ticket) to authorize the `_processors_identity.py` name and the widened 6-function scope, so the leaf is a consistent narrowing of the parent's declared scope rather than a silent redefinition.
evidence: Parent epic 716f-b917-9fc8-41f5 Phase 1: 'graduate the file off BOTH allowlist + ceilings + its architecture.md remedy row'
architecture.md line 277 names the seam '_processors_keys.py' with only '_fold_author_attribution, _rederive_keyring_keys, process_key_event, _bootstrap_genesis_keyring'
Leaf plan: 'DEVIATION from the docs/architecture.md offenders row: that row names the seam _processors_keys.py ... This ticket WIDENS the seam ... and names it _processors_identity.py'
CONFLICT RULE: 'If you believe the parent is genuinely wrong, do NOT silently diverge the leaf — instead update the parent first'
findings.finding: The leaf redefines both the seam name and scope that the parent's architecture.md remedy row specifies: the parent names '_processors_keys.py' with a 4-function cluster, while the leaf delivers '_processors_identity.py' with 6 functions — without first updating the parent to authorize this widening.
findings.location: Plan body ('DEVIATION from the docs/architecture.md offenders row') and docs/architecture.md line 277
scenarios: A reviewer auditing the leaf against the parent's architecture.md row finds the delivered file name and 6-function scope do not match what the parent specifies, making conformance verification ambiguous.
The parent's plan-review attestation is stale relative to the actual seam delivered if the parent's architecture.md row was not updated before this leaf was reviewed.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) open a minimal parent-update to change the architecture.md row from '_processors_keys.py (4 functions)' to '_processors_identity.py (6 functions, including attestation pair, to clear the 100-LOC floor)' and re-attest the parent before this leaf review; or (b) narrow the leaf back to the 4-function '_processors_keys.py' seam and add a follow-on story for the attestation pair once the floor issue is resolved.

findings.finding: The ticket has no `## Acceptance Criteria` checklist. A plan cannot be reviewed for completion without testable, checkable criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `## Acceptance Criteria` section with `- [ ]` checklist items, one per observable, in-session-verifiable outcome.

findings.finding: AC4 requires deletion of the offenders-table row in docs/architecture.md but provides no concrete proving command to verify the row is gone after the edit.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'Verified by: `! grep -q _processors_keys docs/architecture.md` (the stale row is absent).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor the AC4 allowlist grep to the exact filename, e.g. `! grep -q 'reducer/_processors\.py$' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (or match with a word-boundary/path prefix) to avoid false-positive matches on `_processors_identity.py`.
evidence: Taxonomy item 1: substring false-positive — pattern '_processors.py' matches any filename containing that substring, including 'reducer/_processors_identity.py'
The second half of AC4 uses a trailing-space anchor (`reducer/_processors.py `) which correctly avoids this; the first half does not.
findings.finding: AC4's first grep pattern `_processors.py` (without a word-boundary or path anchor) is a substring match that would also match `_processors_identity.py` if that string were ever present in the allowlist, potentially producing a false-positive failure (or masking a true positive if a different _processors variant is added).
scenarios: A future allowlist entry for `reducer/_processors_identity.py` (mistakenly added) would cause `! grep -q '_processors.py' …` to exit 1, falsely failing AC4 even though the original `_processors.py` has been correctly removed.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `! grep -q '_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` with `! grep -qE '(^|/)_processors\.py$' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` or `! grep -q 'reducer/_processors.py' .github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (matching the path-anchored style already used in the second clause).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
